title: SAT vs ACT: Which Test Should You Take (And How to Prepare)
author: Tklesson Team
content:
The SAT and ACT are two of the most important tests you'll take in high school. But which one should you take? And how should you prepare? Here's everything you need to know.

SAT vs ACT: Key Differences

Science Section: ACT has a dedicated science section (35 minutes). SAT does not — science questions are integrated into reading and writing.
Math: SAT gives you a formula sheet; ACT does not. SAT has no-calculator and calculator sections; ACT allows calculator on all math.
Timing: ACT is faster-paced (about 50 seconds per question vs 70 seconds on SAT).
Essay: Both made essays optional. Most colleges don't require them anymore.
Scoring: SAT is 400-1600; ACT is 1-36.

Which Test Should You Take?

Take the SAT if:
• You prefer more time per question
• You're strong in math but weaker in science
• You like that SAT provides formula references
• You prefer questions that require more reasoning than speed

Take the ACT if:
• You work quickly and manage time well
• You're strong in science or like science passages
• You prefer straightforward questions without tricky wording
• You don't mind a faster pace

The best advice: Take a practice test for both (officially released ones). Compare your scores. See which format feels more natural. Many students perform significantly better on one than the other.

When to Start Preparing

Start 3-6 months before your test date. Cramming doesn't work for standardized tests — they measure accumulated skills, not memorized facts. Study consistently, not intensely.

Sophomore year: Take a baseline test to identify weak areas.
Junior year fall: Start serious prep.
Junior year spring: Take your first official test.
Junior year summer: Prep again if needed.
Senior year fall: Final attempt before applications.

How to Study Effectively

Quality over quantity: 30 minutes of focused, mistake-analysis studying beats 2 hours of mindless practice.
Review every mistake: Don't just check your answers. Ask: Why did I miss this? Was it content, timing, or carelessness?
Focus on weak areas: Don't keep practicing what you already know. Target your lowest subscores.
Use official tests: Third-party prep books often don't match the real test's style. Use officially released College Board and ACT practice tests.

Section-by-Section Strategies

Reading: Read the questions first, then the passage. You'll know what to look for. Don't read every word — skim for main ideas and evidence locations.
Writing/Language: Shorter is almost always better on the SAT/ACT. If two answers mean the same thing, pick the more concise one.
Math: Show your work — even on the no-calculator section. Careless errors kill scores. Use answer choices to work backward when you're stuck.
Science (ACT only): Don't read the passages first. Go straight to the questions and look for the data in the graphs and tables. Most answers are right there.

Test Day Tips

• Sleep 8+ hours the night before (this matters more than any last-minute studying)
• Eat a protein-rich breakfast (eggs, Greek yogurt — avoid sugar crashes)
• Bring snacks for breaks (bananas, nuts, dark chocolate)
• Arrive early — running late destroys focus
• Answer every question (no penalty for guessing on SAT and ACT)

Super scoring and Score Choice

Many colleges superscore — they take your highest section scores across multiple test dates. This means you can take the test multiple times and only your best scores count. Check each college's policy.

You've got this. One section at a time. One practice test at a time. The score you want is achievable with consistent effort.
